name: Tilly Foster Iron Mine
short_description: Historic iron mine in the Hudson Highlands with specimens of magnetite, hematite, and garnet. Rockhounding at this abandoned 19th-century mining site offers access to classic metamorphic minerals.
description:
Tilly Foster Iron Mine is a historic mining operation in Putnam County's Hudson Highlands region, known for producing high-quality iron ore during the 19th century. The site exposes metamorphic rocks of the Grenville basement complex, which formed during the Proterozoic orogeny roughly 1 billion years ago. Collectors visiting this location can search for specimens of magnetite, hematite, garnet, and feldspar among the mine tailings and exposed rock faces.

The mining history here dates back to the colonial period, with significant operations during the 1800s when local iron was critical for American industry. The exposed mineralogy reflects the regional metamorphic conditions that transformed the original igneous and sedimentary protoliths into gneiss and schist hosting valuable iron oxides and silicate minerals.

Access to this site requires respect for historical preservation and awareness of hazards associated with abandoned mining infrastructure. The terrain around the mine is rocky and uneven, typical of the Hudson Highlands topography at relatively low elevation. Collectors should focus on mine dumps and accessible outcrop areas rather than entering unstable mine workings.

This location offers a unique opportunity to collect from a site with genuine historical significance while understanding the geological processes that concentrated valuable minerals here. The combination of accessible mineralogy and historic interest makes it a worthwhile destination for collectors interested in New York geology and mining heritage.
history: Tilly Foster Iron Mine operated during the 19th century as part of the Hudson Highlands iron mining district, extracting ore from metamorphic gneiss and schist formed during Grenville orogeny roughly 1 billion years ago. The mine developed along a magnetite-rich zone within Proterozoic basement rocks, making it one of several productive iron mines that supplied raw material to local forges and the broader American industrial economy during the 1800s.
tips: Search the mine dump piles and weathered outcrops for loose magnetite specimens and hematite fragments - the iron oxides are heavy and magnetic, making them easy to spot. Bring a magnet to help identify magnetite in mixed tailings material. Look for well-formed garnet crystals (red to brown) in the metamorphic host rock and along weathered fracture surfaces. Wear sturdy boots with good ankle support since the terrain is rocky and uneven, and avoid approaching any visible mine openings or unstable structures.
terrain_notes: The site sits at roughly 391 feet elevation in the lower Hudson Highlands. Terrain is rocky and uneven with scattered outcrops of metamorphic rock. Vegetation is typical of southeastern New York forests with mixed deciduous trees. The mine area features old tailings piles, exposed rock faces, and areas of loose material. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas ranges from 50 yards to roughly 200 yards depending on specific spots. Footing is difficult due to rocky, uneven ground and loose mine waste material.
written_directions: From the Town of Southeast village center, take NY-311 west toward the Hudson Highlands. Continue roughly 2 miles and watch for the Tilly Foster Mine area markers or access points on the north side of the road. Parking is limited and informal; look for pullouts near any existing access roads. GPS coordinates: 41.412981, -73.642112. Access may be seasonal or restricted - check locally with Putnam County Parks or the Town of Southeast for current access status before visiting.
road_conditions: NY-311 is a paved state road. Access to the mine site itself may involve dirt or gravel pullouts; high-clearance vehicles are not required.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking at pullouts near the mine area. No developed parking lot. Capacity is very limited; early arrival recommended.
collection_rules: Personal collection only. Respect private property and historical site status. Do not enter mine workings or unstable structures. Check with Town of Southeast or Putnam County Parks for any current access restrictions or regulations specific to this historic site. No motorized equipment or blasting. Collect only loose material and surface specimens.
rules:
Personal collection only
No commercial use
Do not enter mine workings or unstable structures
Respect historical preservation status
No motorized equipment
Collect surface specimens and mine dump material only
nearest_services: Town of Southeast village center, roughly 2 miles south. Brewster, NY is roughly 5 miles away with full services including gas, food, and water. Kent Cliffs area also nearby with local services.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose mine tailings material. No wheelchair access. Sturdy boots with good ankle support required. The terrain involves walking over scattered rocks and mine dump piles. Not suitable for limited mobility or young children without supervision. Trails are informal and unmarked.

faq.question: What minerals can I collect at Tilly Foster Iron Mine in New York?
faq.answer: The primary specimens available at Tilly Foster are magnetite, hematite, garnet, and feldspar. Magnetite is the most commonly found - it is black, heavy, and magnetic, making it easy to identify. Red to brown garnet crystals can be found in the metamorphic host rock. Hematite appears as red-brown flakes and masses in the mine dump material.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Tilly Foster Iron Mine near Brewster?
faq.answer: Yes, there is no entrance fee for collecting at Tilly Foster Mine. It is a free public rockhounding site. However, you should verify current access status with the Town of Southeast or Putnam County Parks, as access may be seasonal or subject to restrictions due to the site's historic status.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect specimens at Tilly Foster Mine in Putnam County?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal collection at Tilly Foster. However, the site is a historic mining location, so you should respect any posted restrictions and avoid entering mine workings or unstable structures. Always collect only loose surface material and mine dump specimens.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Tilly Foster Iron Mine?
faq.answer: The best collecting season at Tilly Foster is April through November, when weather conditions are mild and trails are most accessible. Winter months can bring snow and mud, making the rocky terrain more hazardous. Late spring and early fall offer ideal conditions with comfortable temperatures and good visibility.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to collect at Tilly Foster Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a magnet to help identify magnetite in the tailings, a geology hammer or rock pick for sample collection, a backpack, sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support, and a hand lens or magnifying glass to examine specimens. Water and sun protection are essential. Avoid digging or using motorized equipment at this historic site.

faq.question: Is Tilly Foster Iron Mine suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Tilly Foster is beginner-friendly for rockhounders ages 8 and up. The minerals are relatively easy to identify, and specimens are available on mine dumps and surface areas without need for advanced collecting techniques. However, the uneven rocky terrain requires sturdy footwear and adult supervision for young children. Avoid the site during winter when footing is poor.
